title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe (Mole Negro)
seo.metaDescription: Master the rich, complex flavors of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) with our step-by-step recipe. Perfect for a traditional Mexican dinner!

Oaxacan Black Mole step by step
best Oaxacan Black Mole at home

seo.imageAlt: A rich, dark bowl of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole (Mole Negro) with chicken and rice.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the deep, complex flavors of this traditional Oaxacan Black Mole, a true culinary masterpiece.

description: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary masterpiece from Oaxaca, Mexico, known for its incredibly rich, complex, and smoky flavor profile. This traditional sauce, made from a multitude of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and a touch of chocolate, offers a truly unforgettable taste experience. It is often served with chicken or turkey, creating a deeply satisfying and authentic Mexican dinner.

instructions.title: Prepare the Chiles
instructions.text: Lightly toast the dried ancho, mulato, and pasilla chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for 30 seconds per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them. Transfer to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 15-20 minutes until soft. Drain and reserve the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Toast Aromatics and Spices
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast the almonds and sesame seeds until golden. Remove. Then, lightly char the halved tomatoes, chopped onion, and garlic cloves until softened and slightly browned. Add the cinnamon stick, cloves, and peppercorns to the skillet and toast briefly until fragrant, about 30 seconds.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted almonds, sesame seeds, charred tomatoes, onion, garlic, spices, raisins, chopped Mexican chocolate, sliced plantain, and torn corn tortillas in a blender. Add 1 cup of hot chicken broth. Blend until a very smooth paste forms, adding more chicken broth or reserved chile soaking liquid as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. This may take a few minutes and require scraping down the sides.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the lard or vegetable oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot oil. It will sizzle and splatter, so be cautious. Cook, stirring constantly, for 10-15 minutes. This step is crucial for developing the deep flavors of the mole and cooking out the raw chile taste.

instructions.title: Simmer and Season
instructions.text: Gradually stir in the remaining 3 cups of hot chicken broth into the mole paste. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer, then reduce the heat to low. Let the mole simmer, uncovered, for at least 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it thickens to your desired consistency. If it becomes too thick, add a little more broth or water.

instructions.title: Final Adjustments and Serve
instructions.text: Season the mole with salt to taste. If the mole tastes too bitter from the chocolate or chiles, add 1 teaspoon of sugar to balance the flavors. Serve the Mole Negro hot, traditionally with cooked chicken or turkey, rice, and warm tortillas. Garnish with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds.

tips: To ensure a truly smooth mole, pass the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve after blending. This removes any unblended chile skins or seeds.
For a deeper smoky flavor, briefly toast the chiles over an open flame or under a broiler before rehydrating, being careful not to burn them.

Mole Negro can be made ahead of time. Its flavors deepen overnight. Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 5 days, or freeze for up to 3 months. Reheat gently, adding a little broth or water if needed.
article:
Oaxacan Black Mole: A Culinary Masterpiece for Your Dinner Table

Imagine a sauce so rich, so complex, and so deeply flavorful that it tells a story with every bite. This is the magic of Oaxacan Black Mole, often hailed as the king of moles and a crown jewel of Mexican cuisine. Far from a simple sauce, mole negro is a culinary heritage, a labor of love, and a vibrant celebration of Oaxaca’s diverse ingredients. If you’re looking to elevate your Mexican dinner recipes beyond tacos and burritos, delving into the world of Oaxacan Black Mole is an adventure well worth taking.

This isn't just food; it's an experience. Preparing mole negro is a journey through roasting, grinding, and simmering a dizzying array of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and even chocolate, all culminating in a sauce that is smoky, sweet, spicy, and profoundly earthy. While it demands patience and dedication, the reward is an unforgettable meal that will impress even the most discerning palate. Get ready to transform your kitchen into a Oaxacan culinary studio and discover the secrets behind this iconic dish.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole?

At its heart, Oaxacan Black Mole is a symphony of flavors and textures, a dark, velvety sauce that is perhaps the most iconic of Oaxaca's famed "seven moles." Unlike its counterparts, mole negro is distinguished by its intensely dark, almost black color, achieved through the careful charring and roasting of its key ingredients, particularly a specific blend of dried chiles.

Its flavor profile is incredibly intricate: a delicate balance of sweetness from fruits and Mexican chocolate, a smoky depth from toasted chiles and nuts, an earthy base from seeds and spices, and a subtle warmth from various peppers. The complexity comes from its extensive list of components – often 20 to 30 or more – including four types of chiles (mulato, ancho, pasilla Oaxaca, and guajillo), nuts like almonds and peanuts, seeds such as sesame and pumpkin, aromatic spices like cinnamon and cloves, fruits like plantains and raisins, toasted bread or tortillas for thickening, and of course, Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (unrefined cane sugar).

Oaxacan Black Mole is traditionally reserved for special occasions and fiestas, a testament to the time and effort required for its preparation. It’s a dish that embodies hospitality, family, and the rich culinary traditions of Mexico.

## Ingredients for Authentic Mole Negro

Crafting an authentic Oaxacan Black Mole requires a careful selection of ingredients, many of which are toasted or roasted to unlock their full potential. Don't be intimidated by the long list; each component plays a vital role in building the mole's layered flavor.

For the Chiles (approximate quantities for a large batch):
*   6-8 Mulato chiles
*   6-8 Ancho chiles
*   4-6 Pasilla Oaxaca chiles (essential for smoky depth)
*   4-6 Guajillo chiles

For the Aromatics and Thickeners:
*   1 large white onion, roughly chopped
*   8-10 cloves garlic, peeled
*   2 ripe plantains, sliced
*   1/2 cup raisins
*   1/4 cup prunes (pitted)
*   2-3 stale corn tortillas or 1 slice stale bread, torn
*   1 large tomato, roasted
*   4-5 tomatillos, roasted (optional, for a slight tang)

For the Nuts and Seeds:
*   1/2 cup almonds
*   1/4 cup peanuts
*   1/4 cup sesame seeds (+ extra for garnish)
*   2 tablespoons pumpkin seeds (pepitas)

For the Spices:
*   1 teaspoon black peppercorns
*   1/2 teaspoon whole cloves
*   1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
*   1/4 teaspoon anise seeds
*   1 stick Mexican cinnamon (approx. 3-4 inches)
*   Pinch of dried oregano
*   Pinch of dried thyme

For the Sweeteners and Liquids:
*   1 tablet (90g) Mexican chocolate (e.g., Ibarra or Mayordomo)
*   50g piloncillo (or 1/4 cup dark brown sugar)
*   6-8 cups chicken broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian version)
*   1/4 cup lard or vegetable oil
*   Salt to taste

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Oaxacan Black Mole

The journey to a perfect Oaxacan Black Mole begins with the chiles and ends with a rich, velvety sauce. Take your time, and enjoy the process!

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** De-stem and de-seed all the chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the chiles in batches for 30-60 seconds per side until fragrant and slightly pliable, being careful not to burn them (burnt chiles will make the mole bitter). Submerge the toasted chiles in hot water for 20-30 minutes until softened.
2.  **Toast Dry Ingredients:** In the same dry skillet, toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until golden and fragrant. Remove and set aside. Then, toast the peppercorns, cloves, cumin, anise, and cinnamon stick until aromatic. Set aside. Lightly toast the torn tortillas/bread until crisp.
3.  **Sauté Aromatics & Fruits:** Heat a tablespoon of lard or oil in a large pot or skillet. Sauté the chopped onion and garlic until softened and slightly caramelized. Add the sliced plantains, raisins, and prunes, cooking until the plantains are softened and slightly browned. Roast the tomato and tomatillos (if using) until softened and slightly charred.
4.  **Blend the Base:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving some of the soaking liquid. In a high-powered blender, combine the chiles, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, tortillas/bread, sautéed aromatics, fruits, roasted tomato, and tomatillos with 1-2 cups of chicken broth. Blend until incredibly smooth. This may require blending in batches and adding more broth or reserved chile water as needed. For the smoothest mole, you can pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve, though many traditional recipes skip this step.
5.  **Cook the Mole Paste:** Heat the remaining lard or oil in a very large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole paste. This is the most crucial step for developing the mole's deep flavor and dark color. Stir continuously for at least 45 minutes to 1.5 hours. The paste will darken significantly, thicken, and the raw chile flavor will cook out. It will start to pull away from the sides of the pot. Patience is key here!
6.  **Simmer and Season:** Gradually add the remaining chicken broth, stirring well to incorporate. Add the Mexican chocolate and piloncillo, stirring until dissolved. Bring the mole to a gentle simmer and cook for at least 1-2 hours, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld and the sauce to thicken to your desired consistency. If it becomes too thick, add more broth.
7.  **Adjust Seasoning:** Taste and season generously with salt. The mole should have a balanced flavor – sweet, savory, spicy, and earthy.

## Pro Tips for the Best Mole Negro

Achieving the profound depth of flavor in Oaxacan Black Mole isn't just about ingredients; it's about technique and patience.

*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** Toasting chiles releases their oils and enhances flavor, but burning them will make your mole bitter. Toast until fragrant and slightly puffed, then immediately remove from heat.
*   **Invest in a Good Blender:** A powerful blender is essential for achieving a smooth, velvety texture without needing to strain.
*   **The Long Cook is Non-Negotiable:** Stirring the mole paste for an extended period (Step 5) is paramount. This process cooks out raw flavors, deepens the color, and concentrates the complex essences. Do not rush it.
*   **Balance is Key:** Taste frequently throughout the simmering process. You might need to adjust the salt, add a touch more piloncillo for sweetness, or a pinch of salt to brighten the flavors.
*   **Make it Ahead:** Mole negro notoriously tastes even better the day after it's made, as the flavors continue to meld and deepen. It’s an ideal make-ahead dish.
*   **Freezes Beautifully:** Mole freezes wonderfully for up to 3-4 months, making it perfect for batch cooking.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Oaxacan Black Mole is a showstopper on its own, but it truly shines when paired thoughtfully.

*   **Traditional Pairing:** The most classic way to serve Oaxacan Black Mole is with poached or roasted chicken or turkey. The rich sauce beautifully complements the poultry.
*   **Other Proteins:** It also pairs exceptionally well with slow-cooked pork (carnitas), beef short ribs, or even duck.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** For a vegetarian feast, serve mole negro over roasted sweet potatoes, plantains, grilled portobello mushrooms, or as a sauce for enchiladas filled with cheese or vegetables.
*   **Classic Sides:** Always serve with warm corn tortillas for scooping up every last drop, and fluffy white rice to soak up the luxurious sauce.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, finely chopped white onion, and fresh cilantro can add a lovely textural contrast and burst of freshness.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the essence of Oaxacan Black Mole lies in its specific blend of ingredients, you can make minor adjustments based on availability or dietary preferences.

*   **Chiles:** If Pasilla Oaxaca chiles are hard to find, you can increase the mulato and ancho chiles, and consider adding a smoked chipotle chile for a hint of smokiness, though the flavor will differ.
*   **Nuts/Seeds:** You can vary the proportions of nuts and seeds, or substitute with others like pecans or cashews if needed.
*   **Sweeteners:** Dark brown sugar can be used in place of piloncillo.
*   **Chocolate:** If Mexican chocolate is unavailable, use a good quality dark chocolate (60-70% cacao) and adjust the piloncillo/sugar to taste, as Mexican chocolate often contains sugar and cinnamon.
*   **Broth:** Vegetable broth is an excellent substitute for chicken broth to make the mole vegetarian or vegan.
*   **Fat:** Vegetable oil can replace lard.
*   **Fruits:** Dried apricots can be used alongside or instead of raisins and prunes.
*   **Thickeners:** Tortilla chips or even gluten-free bread can be used in place of stale corn tortillas.

Embarking on the creation of Oaxacan Black Mole is more than just cooking; it's an immersive culinary experience that connects you to generations of tradition. The effort required is a testament to the love poured into this dish, and the resulting flavor is a profound reward. So, gather your ingredients, clear your schedule, and prepare to create a Mexican dinner recipe that will be remembered long after the last bite.
